Key Finding: Both treatment groups improved vs control; PACVS patients explicitly included alongside PACS
Single neuraltherapeutic stimulus sufficient to completely reverse persistent post-vaccination musculoskeletal pain

Evidence Type: Case series n=18 (pre-selected responders) + Phase III RCT ongoing
Key Finding: Biomarker normalisation (CD16+ monocytes) + subjective symptom improvement; critical selection bias — responders only enrolled

Therapy: Vaxtherapy protocol (nattokinase / fibrinolytics → monoclonal antibody → antimicrobials → mitochondrial supplements)
Key Finding: Four-stage protocol anchored in pathophysiology; stage 4 (mitochondrial supplements) directly overlaps with VitalScan4PACVS protocol

Evidence Type: Prospective observational n=14 treated vs 13 controls 20 days
Key Finding: 29% symptom-free at 20 days; fatigue, tachycardia, abdominal symptoms significantly lower in treated group; not statistically significant (underpowered)

Evidence Type: Observational n=26 of 49 long COVID patients 4–16 weeks
Key Finding: Gradual symptom improvement over 4–16 weeks; gradual response argues against placebo; T cell alterations documented
H1+H2 antihistamine RCT in long COVID; results pending publication

Evidence Type: Case series n=3 NAC vs n=6 controls + medrxiv RCT (acute COVID)
Key Finding: Yale: vWF normalised 3/3 NAC vs 0/6 controls (p=0.0119); primary mechanism is lipid metabolism restoration (beta-oxidation protection) not spike protein
Fatigue 8.7% vs 80.1% placebo (p<0.0001); 6MWT d=0.56; eNOS/ADMA ratio d=0.72; targets documented arginine depletion in PASC
Mental fatigue d=1.02 (6mo); concentration d=1.19–2.46 (note: n=6 inflates effect sizes); 2026 RCT: fatigue −2.05 points p=0.005; ATP buffering + neuroprotection mechanism

Evidence Type: Pilot trial (NAD+ + LDN combined) + null NR RCT
Key Finding: Isman 2024: Chalder fatigue 25.9→17.4 (p<0.0001) but combined with LDN (cannot isolate); dedicated NR RCT: no significant effect vs placebo; NAD+ depletion well documented in PASC
Citrulline/ornithine ratio elevated in PASC; bypasses hepatic arginine metabolism to raise plasma arginine more effectively than arginine alone; no PASC-specific RCT
Depleted in PASC (López-Hernández 2023); acute COVID RCT: reduced hospitalisation duration (Cengiz 2020); TCA cycle alternative substrate when beta-oxidation impaired; no PASC-specific RCT
Khoramjoo 2024: plasma taurine inversely correlated with PASC severity (148 vs 225 μM); 1.6g/day raised plasma 34μM; mitochondrial tRNA modification + NF-κB inhibition; no PASC intervention RCT

Evidence Type: PASC RCT null (1g/day) + acylcarnitine depletion data
Key Finding: Scaturro 2022: no effect at 1g/day in PASC; acylcarnitines accumulate in PASC plasma (Guntur 2022) consistent with beta-oxidation transport bottleneck; included at 2g based on dose-response reasoning

Evidence Type: Depletion data with symptom correlation + healthy volunteer RCT
Key Finding: Saito 2024: depleted in PASC; inversely correlated with neuropsychiatric symptoms; D-serine (NMDA co-agonist) improves cognition in healthy volunteers; no PASC RCT
Significant improvement in physical and cognitive fatigue vs placebo at 4 weeks; validates beta-oxidation as therapeutic target in long COVID; not commercially available
Clinician-diagnosed long COVID halved at 6 months (RR 0.495; 95% CI 0.155–0.995); prevention not treatment; concordance across two independent trials strengthens signal
No significant symptom improvement in either trial; viral persistence as sole causal mechanism for established long COVID not supported by RCT evidence

Evidence Type: Meta-analysis of 4 pre-post studies (n=~150) + 3 RCTs ongoing
Key Finding: Hedges' g = −0.74 on fatigue (p<0.001); no RCTs published to date; TRPM3 ion channel restoration in NK cells proposed mechanism; BC RCT (n=160) expected soon

Evidence Type: Case series n=6–9 + Phase 2 RCT ongoing (n=200) + RECOVER-AUTO
Key Finding: ~50% of case series participants reported near-normal recovery; autoantibody neutralisation proposed mechanism; elevated GPCR autoantibodies documented in PACVS (Mundorf 2024)
Fibrinolytic enzyme trial for microclot dissolution; no results yet; most rigorous fibrinolytic trial in long COVID to date; PolyBio Research Foundation

Hulscher/McCullough 2023: proposed spike protein degradation via fibrinolysis and proteolysis; no published clinical trial in PASC/PACVS; widely promoted in medical freedom communities — evidence significantly overstated

Evidence Type: RCT n=328 across 22 US sites (RECOVER initiative)
Key Finding: None of three cognitive rehabilitation interventions proved effective vs comparator; all five arms showed modest improvements over time; important null result for cognitive rehabilitation in long COVID
Cognitive and affective psychoeducation interventions for long COVID cognitive complaints; results published 2025
Significant HR reduction p=0.028; significant symptom burden reduction p=0.007; best pharmacological RCT evidence for POTS; applicable to post-COVID POTS phenotype

Evidence Type: 55% observational success rate + COVIVA nested RCT (NCT05481177)
Key Finding: Consistent HR reduction across studies; selective If channel blocker — HR reduction without other cardiovascular effects; COVIVA published Frontiers Neurol July 2025

Evidence Type: Systematic review 32 studies + observational n series
Key Finding: 33.9% treatment success in observational data; adequate evidence for hemodynamic benefit; moderate evidence per VA Long COVID guidelines
Blood volume expansion; 42.8% success in observational data; low evidence per VA Long COVID guidelines; used for low blood pressure phenotype
Beneficial effect on hemodynamics in POTS; particularly relevant for hyperadrenergic phenotype; 21-RCT systematic review (2025)
Improved lung function, physical activity, wellbeing in long COVID without PEM; must start recumbent (rowing/swimming/cycling); critical PEM caveat; Karolinska RCT results pending 2025
Standard POTS supportive measure; 2–3L fluid/day + 3–5g sodium/day; minimally adequate evidence per guidelines; widely recommended as first-line non-pharmacological
Did not change HR increment but significantly reduced symptom burden score (VOSS 13 vs 19; p=0.009); nocturia risk at higher doses
Significant improvements in itching, flushing, skin reactivity, headache, tachycardia, and quality of life vs placebo; mastocytosis population — applicable to MCAS by shared mechanism
Primarily for GI symptoms; mast cell stabiliser; 200mg 4x daily before meals; 4–8 week onset before judging effect; poor systemic absorption but GI benefit reported

2nd most commonly used drug after antihistamines in idiopathic MCAS; RCT evidence from urticaria (34 RCTs) shows modest benefit of adding LTRA to anti-H1; cysteinyl leukotriene elevation documented during MCAS episodes

Evidence Type: Long clinical track record + observational MCAS series
Key Finding: Dual H1 blocker + mast cell stabiliser; 1–2mg twice daily; 4–8 weeks to full effect; used when antihistamines alone insufficient; no MCAS-specific RCT
For prostaglandin-mediated MCAS (flushing, hypotension, PGD2 elevation); must be introduced in controlled setting — risk of triggering MC degranulation; 81mg daily typical dose
